full_context: According to GB 50009-2012 "Load Code for the Design of Building Structures", Appendix E, the characteristic value of wind load can be calculated by Formula (E.1): w_k = \beta_{gz} \mu_s \mu_z w_0. Where: w_k is the characteristic value of wind load, kN/m²; \beta_{gz} is the gust coefficient at height z; \mu_s is the shape coefficient of wind load; \mu_z is the height variation coefficient of wind pressure; w_0 is the basic wind pressure, kN/m². This formula is used for wind-resistant design of building structures.

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic value of wind load
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard value of wind pressure acting on the building surface

variable_definitions.name: Gust coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering fluctuating wind pressure

variable_definitions.name: Shape coefficient of wind load
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient reflecting the influence of building shape on wind pressure distribution

variable_definitions.name: Height variation coefficient of wind pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering wind speed variation with height

variable_definitions.name: Basic wind pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Basic wind pressure at standard height

potential_questions: Given basic wind pressure w_0=0.45 kN/m², gust coefficient \beta_{gz}=1.65 at height z=50 m, shape coefficient \mu_s=1.3, height variation coefficient \mu_z=1.67, calculate the characteristic value of wind load w_k.
task_description: 1. Obtain w_0, \beta_{gz}, \mu_s, \mu_z; 2. Calculate w_k = \beta_{gz} × \mu_s × \mu_z × w_0; 3. Output w_k.

full_context: According to GB 50010-2010 "Code for Design of Concrete Structures", Clause 6.2.10, the relative limit compressive height can be calculated by Formula (6.2.10-1): \xi_b = \beta_1/(1 + f_y/(E_s \varepsilon_{cu})). Where: \xi_b is the relative limit compressive height; \beta_1 is a coefficient, taken as 0.8 when concrete strength grade ≤ C50; f_y is the design tensile strength of steel bar, MPa; E_s is the elastic modulus of steel bar, MPa; \varepsilon_{cu} is the ultimate compressive strain of concrete, taken as 0.0033. This parameter is used to distinguish between under-reinforced and over-reinforced beams.

variable_definitions.name: Relative limit compressive height
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of limit compressive height to effective height between under-reinforced and over-reinforced beams

variable_definitions.name: Coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient related to concrete strength grade

variable_definitions.name: Design tensile strength of steel bar
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable tensile strength of steel bar in design

variable_definitions.name: Elastic modulus of steel bar
variable_definitions.meaning: Stress-strain ratio of steel bar in elastic stage

variable_definitions.name: Ultimate compressive strain of concrete
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate strain when concrete fails in compression

potential_questions: Given concrete grade C30 (\beta_1=0.8), HRB400 steel bar (f_y=360 MPa, E_s=2.0×10⁵ MPa), \varepsilon_{cu}=0.0033, calculate the relative limit compressive height \xi_b.
task_description: 1. Obtain \beta_1, f_y, E_s, \varepsilon_{cu}; 2. Calculate f_y/(E_s \varepsilon_{cu}); 3. Calculate 1 + f_y/(E_s \varepsilon_{cu}); 4. Calculate \xi_b = \beta_1 / (1 + f_y/(E_s \varepsilon_{cu})); 5. Output \xi_b.

full_context: According to GB 50007-2011 "Code for Design of Building Foundations", Clause 5.2.4, the modified characteristic value of subsoil bearing capacity can be calculated by Formula (5.2.4): f_a = f_{ak} + \eta_b \gamma (b-3) + \eta_d \gamma_m (d-0.5). Where: f_a is the modified characteristic value of subsoil bearing capacity, kPa; f_{ak} is the characteristic value of subsoil bearing capacity, kPa; \eta_b, \eta_d are correction coefficients for foundation width and depth; \gamma is the unit weight of soil below foundation base, kN/m³; b is foundation width, m; \gamma_m is weighted average unit weight of soil above foundation base, kN/m³; d is foundation embedment depth, m. This formula considers the influence of foundation size and embedment depth on bearing capacity.

variable_definitions.name: Modified characteristic value of subsoil bearing capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Subsoil bearing capacity modified by foundation size and depth

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic value of subsoil bearing capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Bearing capacity determined by load test or empirical formula

variable_definitions.name: Width correction coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient for foundation width effect

variable_definitions.name: Depth correction coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient for foundation depth effect

variable_definitions.name: Unit weight of soil below base
variable_definitions.meaning: Natural unit weight of soil below foundation base

variable_definitions.name: Foundation width
variable_definitions.meaning: Short side width of foundation base

variable_definitions.name: Weighted average unit weight
variable_definitions.meaning: Weighted average unit weight of soil layers above foundation base

variable_definitions.name: Foundation embedment depth
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from foundation base to outdoor design ground

potential_questions: Given f_{ak}=180 kPa, \eta_b=0.3, \eta_d=1.5, \gamma=18 kN/m³, b=2.5 m, \gamma_m=17 kN/m³, d=1.8 m, calculate the modified bearing capacity f_a.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters; 2. Calculate \eta_b \gamma (b-3); 3. Calculate \eta_d \gamma_m (d-0.5); 4. Calculate f_a = f_{ak} + result2 + result3; 5. Output f_a.

full_context: According to GB 50017-2017 "Standard for Design of Steel Structures", Clause 4.1.1, the strength of members under biaxial bending can be checked by Formula (4.1.1): M_x/(\gamma_x W_{nx}) + M_y/(\gamma_y W_{ny}) ≤ f. Where: M_x, M_y are bending moments about x and y axes at the same section, N·mm; W_{nx}, W_{ny} are net section moduli about x and y axes, mm³; \gamma_x, \gamma_y are plastic development coefficients; f is design flexural strength of steel, MPa. This formula applies to I-shaped, box sections under biaxial bending.

variable_definitions.name: Bending moment
variable_definitions.meaning: Design bending moment about x and y axes

variable_definitions.name: Net section modulus
variable_definitions.meaning: Section modulus considering hole weakening

variable_definitions.name: Plastic development coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering partial section plasticity

variable_definitions.name: Design flexural strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Design strength of steel under bending

potential_questions: Given I-shaped steel beam: M_x=80 kN·m, M_y=15 kN·m, W_{nx}=1000 cm³, W_{ny}=200 cm³, \gamma_x=1.05, \gamma_y=1.2, f=215 MPa, check whether strength is satisfied.
task_description: 1. Unify units: convert moment to N·mm, section modulus to mm³; 2. Calculate M_x/(\gamma_x W_{nx}); 3. Calculate M_y/(\gamma_y W_{ny}); 4. Sum the two values; 5. Compare with f; 6. Output result (satisfied/unsatisfied).

full_context: According to GB 50011-2010 "Code for Seismic Design of Buildings", Clause 5.1.4, the characteristic value of total horizontal seismic action can be calculated by Formula (5.1.4-1): F_{Ek} = \alpha_1 G_{eq}. Where: F_{Ek} is characteristic value of total horizontal seismic action, kN; \alpha_1 is horizontal seismic influence coefficient corresponding to fundamental natural period; G_{eq} is equivalent total gravity load of structure, kN. This formula is the basis of the base shear method.

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic value of total horizontal seismic action
variable_definitions.meaning: Total seismic shear at structure base

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal seismic influence coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient related to natural period and site condition

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent total gravity load
variable_definitions.meaning: Representative value of gravity load for multi-mass system simplified to single mass

potential_questions: Given fundamental period T₁=0.8 s, 8-degree zone (0.20g), design seismic group 1, Class Ⅱ site, \alpha₁=0.16, G_{eq}=85000 kN, calculate total horizontal seismic action F_{Ek}.
task_description: 1. Obtain \alpha_1 and G_{eq}; 2. Calculate F_{Ek} = \alpha_1 × G_{eq}; 3. Output F_{Ek}.

full_context: According to GB 50003-2011 "Code for Design of Masonry Structures", Clause 5.1.1, the slenderness ratio of masonry walls and columns can be calculated by Formula (5.1.1): \beta = H_0 / h. Where: \beta is slenderness ratio; H_0 is calculated height of wall or column, mm; h is wall thickness or corresponding side length of rectangular column, mm. Slenderness ratio check is important for stability of masonry structures.

variable_definitions.name: Slenderness ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of calculated height to section thickness

variable_definitions.name: Calculated height
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent height considering support constraints

variable_definitions.name: Section thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Wall thickness or column dimension in calculated height direction

potential_questions: Given calculated height H_0=3600 mm, wall thickness h=240 mm, calculate slenderness ratio \beta.
task_description: 1. Obtain H_0 and h; 2. Calculate \beta = H_0/h; 3. Output \beta.

full_context: According to GB 50014-2006 "Code for Design of Outdoor Wastewater Engineering", Clause 4.2.1, discharge of uniform open-channel flow can be calculated by Manning formula: Q = (1/n) A R^{2/3} I^{1/2}. Where: Q is discharge, m³/s; n is roughness coefficient; A is cross-sectional flow area, m²; R is hydraulic radius, m; I is hydraulic slope. This formula is widely used in drainage pipe and open-channel design.

variable_definitions.name: Discharge
variable_definitions.meaning: Water volume per unit time through cross-section

variable_definitions.name: Roughness coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient reflecting channel surface roughness

variable_definitions.name: Cross-sectional flow area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area perpendicular to flow direction

variable_definitions.name: Hydraulic radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of flow area to wetted perimeter

variable_definitions.name: Hydraulic slope
variable_definitions.meaning: Head loss per unit length

potential_questions: Given rectangular drainage channel width b=2 m, water depth h=1.2 m, n=0.013, I=0.001, calculate discharge Q.
task_description: 1. Calculate A = b × h; 2. Calculate wetted perimeter P = b + 2h; 3. Calculate R = A/P; 4. Calculate R^{2/3}; 5. Calculate I^{1/2}; 6. Calculate Q = (1/n) × A × R^{2/3} × I^{1/2}; 7. Output Q.
discipline: Civil Engineering/Water Supply and Drainage Engineering

full_context: According to GB 50019-2015 "Code for Design of Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning of Industrial Buildings", Clause 6.2.1, heating load can be estimated by Formula (6.2.1): Q = c_p \rho L (t_n - t_w). Where: Q is heating load, W; c_p is specific heat at constant pressure of air, J/(kg·K); \rho is air density, kg/m³; L is ventilation rate, m³/s; t_n is indoor design temperature, ℃; t_w is outdoor design temperature, ℃. This formula is used for ventilation heating load calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Heating load
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat required to maintain indoor design temperature

variable_definitions.name: Specific heat at constant pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat required to raise air temperature by 1K at constant pressure

variable_definitions.name: Air density
variable_definitions.meaning: Mass per unit volume of air

variable_definitions.name: Ventilation rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Volume of air entering room per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Indoor design temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Indoor temperature maintained by heating system

variable_definitions.name: Outdoor design temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Outdoor calculation temperature for heating

potential_questions: Given ventilation rate L=10 m³/s, t_n=18 ℃, t_w=-10 ℃, \rho=1.2 kg/m³, c_p=1005 J/(kg·K), calculate heating load Q.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters; 2. Calculate temperature difference Δt = t_n - t_w; 3. Calculate Q = c_p × \rho × L × Δt; 4. Output Q.

full_context: According to GB 50015-2019 "Standard for Design of Building Water Supply and Drainage", Clause 3.6.4, design flow rate of domestic water supply pipe for residential building can be calculated by Formula (3.6.4): q_g = 0.2 \alpha \sqrt{N_g}. Where: q_g is design flow rate of pipe section, L/s; \alpha is coefficient determined by building usage; N_g is total number of water supply fixtures. This formula is used for hydraulic calculation of residential water supply system.

variable_definitions.name: Design flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Flow rate calculated for maximum instantaneous water consumption

variable_definitions.name: Coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient based on building type and water usage pattern

variable_definitions.name: Total number of water supply fixtures
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of standard equivalent fixtures

potential_questions: Given total fixture count N_g=30, \alpha=2.5, calculate design flow rate q_g.
task_description: 1. Obtain \alpha and N_g; 2. Calculate \sqrt{N_g}; 3. Calculate q_g = 0.2 × \alpha × \sqrt{N_g}; 4. Output q_g.
discipline: Civil Engineering/Water Supply and Drainage Engineering

full_context: According to GB 50016-2014 "Code for Design of Building Fire Protection", Clause 10.3.1, emergency lighting illuminance can be calculated by Formula (10.3.1): E_{av} = (\Phi N K U)/A. Where: E_{av} is average illuminance, lx; \Phi is luminous flux of single lamp, lm; N is number of lamps; K is maintenance factor; U is utilization factor; A is illuminated area, m². This formula is used for emergency lighting system design.

variable_definitions.name: Average illuminance
variable_definitions.meaning: Average luminous flux density on illuminated surface

variable_definitions.name: Luminous flux
variable_definitions.meaning: Total visible light power emitted by light source

variable_definitions.name: Number of lamps
variable_definitions.meaning: Total lamps installed in lighting area

variable_definitions.name: Maintenance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor considering lamp aging and dust

variable_definitions.name: Utilization factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of luminous flux reaching working surface to total flux

variable_definitions.name: Illuminated area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area requiring lighting

potential_questions: Given emergency lamp flux \Phi=2000 lm, N=8 lamps, K=0.7, U=0.6, A=120 m², calculate average illuminance E_{av}.
task_description: 1. Obtain \Phi, N, K, U, A; 2. Calculate \Phi × N × K × U; 3. Calculate E_{av} = (\Phi × N × K × U)/A; 4. Output E_{av}.

full_context: According to GB 50028-2006 "Code for Design of City Gas Engineering", Clause 6.3.1, frictional pressure loss of gas pipeline can be calculated by Darcy-Weisbach formula: \Delta P = \lambda (L/d) (\rho v²/2). Where: \Delta P is frictional pressure loss, Pa; \lambda is friction coefficient; L is pipeline length, m; d is inner diameter, m; \rho is gas density, kg/m³; v is gas velocity, m/s. This formula is used for hydraulic calculation of gas distribution system.

variable_definitions.name: Frictional pressure loss
variable_definitions.meaning: Pressure loss due to friction in straight pipe

variable_definitions.name: Friction coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Dimensionless coefficient related to Reynolds number and roughness

variable_definitions.name: Pipeline length
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual length of calculated pipe segment

variable_definitions.name: Inner diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal diameter of pipeline

variable_definitions.name: Gas density
variable_definitions.meaning: Gas density under working condition

variable_definitions.name: Gas velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Average flow velocity in pipeline

potential_questions: Given L=500 m, d=0.2 m, \lambda=0.02, \rho=0.75 kg/m³, v=10 m/s, calculate frictional pressure loss \Delta P.
task_description: 1. Obtain \lambda, L, d, \rho, v; 2. Calculate L/d; 3. Calculate \rho v²/2; 4. Calculate \Delta P = \lambda × (L/d) × (\rho v²/2); 5. Output \Delta P.

full_context: According to GB 50029-2014 "Code for Design of Compressed Air Stations", Clause 4.2.1, cylinder displacement of air compressor can be calculated by Formula (4.2.1): V = Q/(\eta_v n). Where: V is cylinder displacement, m³; Q is compressor discharge, m³/min; \eta_v is volumetric efficiency; n is compressor speed, r/min. This formula is used for compressor selection and design.

variable_definitions.name: Cylinder displacement
variable_definitions.meaning: Swept volume of piston in one stroke

variable_definitions.name: Discharge
variable_definitions.meaning: Gas volume discharged per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Volumetric efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge

variable_definitions.name: Compressor speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Crankshaft revolutions per minute

potential_questions: Given Q=10 m³/min, \eta_v=0.85, n=980 r/min, calculate cylinder displacement V.
task_description: 1. Obtain Q, \eta_v, n; 2. Calculate V = Q/(\eta_v × n); 3. Output V.

full_context: According to GB 50034-2013 "Standard for Lighting Design of Buildings", Clause 4.1.2, average indoor lighting illuminance can be calculated by lumen method: E = (\Phi N K U)/A. Where: E is average illuminance, lx; \Phi is luminous flux of single lamp, lm; N is number of lamps; K is maintenance factor; U is utilization factor; A is illuminated area, m². This is the basic method for lighting design.

variable_definitions.name: Average illuminance
variable_definitions.meaning: Average illuminance on indoor illuminated surface

variable_definitions.name: Luminous flux
variable_definitions.meaning: Luminous flux of single lamp

variable_definitions.name: Number of lamps
variable_definitions.meaning: Total installed lamps

variable_definitions.name: Maintenance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Reduction factor considering light source and lamp condition

variable_definitions.name: Utilization factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of flux reaching working surface to total flux

variable_definitions.name: Illuminated area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area requiring lighting

potential_questions: Given LED lamp flux \Phi=3000 lm, N=12 lamps, K=0.8, U=0.7, A=60 m², calculate average illuminance E.
task_description: 1. Obtain \Phi, N, K, U, A; 2. Calculate \Phi × N × K × U; 3. Calculate E = (\Phi × N × K × U)/A; 4. Output E.

full_context: According to GB 50057-2010 "Code for Protection of Structures Against Lightning", Appendix C, grounding resistance of vertical grounding electrode can be calculated by Formula (C.1): R = \rho/(2π L) · ln[(L+√(L²+r²))/r]. Where: R is grounding resistance, Ω; \rho is soil resistivity, Ω·m; L is length of grounding electrode, m; r is equivalent radius of grounding electrode, m. This formula is used for lightning protection grounding system design.

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistance between grounding electrode and remote earth

variable_definitions.name: Soil resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Physical quantity characterizing soil conductivity

variable_definitions.name: Length of grounding electrode
variable_definitions.meaning: Buried length of vertical grounding electrode

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent cylindrical radius of grounding electrode

potential_questions: Given soil resistivity \rho=100 Ω·m, L=2.5 m, r=0.02 m, calculate grounding resistance R.
task_description: 1. Obtain \rho, L, r; 2. Calculate L² and r²; 3. Calculate √(L²+r²); 4. Calculate L+√(L²+r²); 5. Calculate ln[(L+√(L²+r²))/r]; 6. Calculate R = \rho/(2π L) × ln[(L+√(L²+r²))/r]; 7. Output R.

full_context: According to GB 50068-2018 "Unified Standard for Reliability Design of Building Structures", Clause 3.2.1, structural members designed for ultimate limit state shall satisfy Formula (3.2.1): \gamma_0 S ≤ R. Where: \gamma_0 is structural importance factor; S is design value of action effect combination; R is design value of member resistance. This formula is the basic principle of structural design.

variable_definitions.name: Structural importance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor considering reliability requirement based on safety class

variable_definitions.name: Design value of action effect combination
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal force from load combination

variable_definitions.name: Design value of member resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal force that member can bear

potential_questions: Given \gamma_0=1.0, bending moment S=150 kN·m, bending resistance R=180 kN·m, check whether ultimate limit state is satisfied.
task_description: 1. Obtain \gamma_0, S, R; 2. Calculate \gamma_0 × S; 3. Compare \gamma_0 S and R; 4. If \gamma_0 S ≤ R, satisfied; else not satisfied; 5. Output result.

full_context: According to GB 50009-2012 "Load Code for the Design of Building Structures", Clause 3.2.3, design value of load effect for fundamental combination can be calculated by Formula (3.2.3-1): S_d = \gamma_G S_{Gk} + \gamma_{Q1} S_{Q1k} + Σ_{i=2}^n \gamma_{Qi} \psi_{ci} S_{Qik}. Where: S_d is design value of load effect combination; \gamma_G is partial factor for permanent load; S_{Gk} is characteristic value of permanent load effect; \gamma_{Q1}, \gamma_{Qi} are partial factors for variable loads; S_{Q1k}, S_{Qik} are characteristic values of variable load effects; \psi_{ci} is combination coefficient for variable loads. This formula is used for strength calculation of structural members.

variable_definitions.name: Design value of load effect combination
variable_definitions.meaning: Load effect considering partial factors and combination coefficients

variable_definitions.name: Partial factor for permanent load
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor considering variability of permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic value of permanent load effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal force from permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Partial factor for variable load
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor considering variability of variable load

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic value of variable load effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal force from variable load

variable_definitions.name: Combination coefficient for variable load
variable_definitions.meaning: Reduction factor for simultaneous occurrence probability

potential_questions: Given permanent moment M_{Gk}=50 kN·m, live load moment M_{Q1k}=30 kN·m, wind moment M_{Q2k}=20 kN·m, \gamma_G=1.2, \gamma_{Q1}=1.4, \gamma_{Q2}=1.4, \psi_{c2}=0.6, calculate fundamental combination moment M_d.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters; 2. Calculate \gamma_G × M_{Gk}; 3. Calculate \gamma_{Q1} × M_{Q1k}; 4. Calculate \gamma_{Q2} × \psi_{c2} × M_{Q2k}; 5. Calculate M_d = result2 + result3 + result4; 6. Output M_d.

full_context: According to GB 50010-2010 "Code for Design of Concrete Structures", Clause 6.2.17, axial compressive capacity of normal section can be calculated by Formula (6.2.17): N ≤ 0.9\varphi (f_c A + f_y' A_s'). Where: N is design axial compression, N; \varphi is stability coefficient of reinforced concrete member; f_c is design axial compressive strength of concrete, MPa; A is section area, mm²; f_y' is design compressive strength of longitudinal steel, MPa; A_s' is total area of longitudinal steel, mm². This formula is used for column, pier and other compression members.

variable_definitions.name: Design axial compression
variable_definitions.meaning: Pressure acting along member axis

variable_definitions.name: Stability coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering slenderness effect on capacity

variable_definitions.name: Design axial compressive strength of concrete
variable_definitions.meaning: Compressive strength of concrete under axial load

variable_definitions.name: Section area
variable_definitions.meaning: Total cross-sectional area of member

variable_definitions.name: Design compressive strength of steel
variable_definitions.meaning: Compressive strength of steel bar

variable_definitions.name: Area of longitudinal steel
variable_definitions.meaning: Total area of longitudinal compression steel

potential_questions: Given 400×400 mm column, C30 concrete (f_c=14.3 MPa), 8Φ20 bars (A_s'=2513 mm², f_y'=360 MPa), \varphi=0.95, calculate design axial compressive capacity.
task_description: 1. Calculate A = 400×400; 2. Calculate f_c × A; 3. Calculate f_y' × A_s'; 4. Calculate f_c A + f_y' A_s'; 5. Calculate 0.9 × \varphi × (f_c A + f_y' A_s'); 6. Output design capacity.

full_context: According to GB 50007-2011 "Code for Design of Building Foundations", Clause 8.2.7, punching shear capacity of isolated foundation slab shall satisfy Formula (8.2.7-1): p_j ≤ 1.2 f_a. Where: p_j is net reaction per unit area under basic load combination, kPa; f_a is design axial tensile strength of concrete, kPa. This formula is used to determine foundation slab thickness.

variable_definitions.name: Net foundation reaction
variable_definitions.meaning: Base reaction deducting self-weight and overlying soil

variable_definitions.name: Design axial tensile strength of concrete
variable_definitions.meaning: Tensile strength of concrete under axial load

potential_questions: Given net reaction p_j=250 kPa, C25 concrete (f_t=1.27 MPa=1270 kPa), check whether punching shear is satisfied.
task_description: 1. Obtain p_j and f_t; 2. Calculate 1.2 × f_t; 3. Compare p_j and 1.2 × f_t; 4. If p_j ≤ 1.2 × f_t, satisfied; else not satisfied; 5. Output result.
section: 8.2.7 Punching Shear Calculation of Isolated Foundation

full_context: According to GB 50017-2017 "Standard for Design of Steel Structures", Clause 7.2.1, shear strength of flexural member can be checked by Formula (7.2.1): \tau = V S/(I t_w) ≤ f_v. Where: \tau is shear stress, MPa; V is shear force at web plane, N; S is area moment above shear stress calculation position about neutral axis, mm³; I is moment of inertia of gross section, mm⁴; t_w is web thickness, mm; f_v is design shear strength of steel, MPa. This formula is used for web shear check of steel beams.

variable_definitions.name: Shear stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Shear stress on section

variable_definitions.name: Shear force
variable_definitions.meaning: Design shear force at calculated section

variable_definitions.name: Area moment
variable_definitions.meaning: Static moment above shear position about neutral axis

variable_definitions.name: Moment of inertia
variable_definitions.meaning: Moment of inertia of gross section about neutral axis

variable_definitions.name: Web thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Thickness of beam web

variable_definitions.name: Design shear strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Shear strength of steel

potential_questions: Given I-shaped beam: V=300 kN, S=500×10³ mm³, I=20000×10⁴ mm⁴, t_w=10 mm, Q235 steel (f_v=125 MPa), check shear strength.
task_description: 1. Unify units: convert V to N; 2. Calculate \tau = (V × S)/(I × t_w); 3. Compare \tau and f_v; 4. If \tau ≤ f_v, satisfied; else not satisfied; 5. Output result.

full_context: According to GB 50011-2010 "Code for Seismic Design of Buildings", Clause 5.4.1, fundamental combination of seismic action and other loads shall satisfy Formula (5.4.1): S ≤ R/\gamma_{RE}. Where: S is design internal force of member; R is design resistance of member; \gamma_{RE} is seismic adjustment factor (related to member type, ductility and design value). This formula is used for bearing capacity check under seismic action.

variable_definitions.name: Design internal force
variable_definitions.meaning: Internal force from combination of seismic and other loads

variable_definitions.name: Design resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Bearing capacity of member under seismic action

variable_definitions.name: Seismic adjustment factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Adjustment factor for seismic bearing capacity check

potential_questions: Given seismic combination moment S=220 kN·m, bending resistance R=280 kN·m, \gamma_{RE}=1.1, check whether requirement is satisfied.
task_description: 1. Obtain S, R, \gamma_{RE}; 2. Calculate R/\gamma_{RE}; 3. Compare S and R/\gamma_{RE}; 4. If S ≤ R/\gamma_{RE}, satisfied; else not satisfied; 5. Output result.
section: 5.4.1 Bearing Capacity Check Under Seismic Action
